items.myth: Deep networks fail because optimizers get trapped in bad local minima with high loss.
items.reality: In very high dimensions, saddles and flat regions often dominate critical points more than bad local minima; many successful runs reach low training loss. Initialization, learning rate, and noise (SGD) shape which basin you enter.
items.example: A ResNet on CIFAR-10 often reaches near-zero training error from standard init—suggesting the obstacle is finding a good generalizing basin, not escaping a high-loss local minimum.

items.myth: The loss surface is a fixed physical object independent of batch or data sampling.
items.reality: L(θ) is defined over a dataset; mini-batch training evaluates stochastic estimates of that surface. Full-batch and mini-batch landscapes are related but not identical step-to-step.
items.example: With batch size 32, each step uses a noisy estimate of the full-data surface; the optimizer walks on a slightly jittered terrain compared to full-batch gradient descent.

items.myth: If you cannot draw the surface, the landscape metaphor is useless for large models.
items.reality: Local concepts—gradient direction, curvature, plateaus, valley width—remain meaningful in high dimensions and explain learning-rate tuning, momentum, and sharp vs flat minima.
items.example: Loss barely changes for 10 epochs because you are on a plateau (small ||∇L|| everywhere nearby), even though you cannot plot all 10 million parameters.
